name: Smart-Its
short_description: A platform of small embedded modules that add sensing, processing, and wireless communication to any everyday artifact, turning it into a networked smart object.
long_description: Smart-Its were postage-stamp-scale electronic modules that could be attached post hoc to ordinary objects, augmenting them with multi-sensor perception, a microcontroller, and short-range wireless networking so artifacts could perceive their situation and exchange information. Developed under the EU Disappearing Computer initiative, the platform embodied an artifact-centered vision of ubiquitous computing in which computation recedes into the physical world rather than appearing as a screen-based device. Its best-known interaction technique, Smart-Its Friends, let a user pair two devices simply by holding them together and shaking them, with the modules detecting their shared motion via correlated accelerometer signals. The work is a foundational example of tangible, embedded, context-aware interaction and a precursor to today's Internet of Things.
ai_note: Smart-Its rely on embedded sensing, microcontroller processing, and signal-correlation matching (e.g. accelerometer coherence for shake-pairing), not on any machine-learning model running inside the system.

images.caption: Smart-Its embedded sensor module attached to an everyday artifact, as presented in the Communications of the ACM article.
images.alt_text: A small circuit board module with sensors and a microcontroller, designed to be attached to an everyday object to give it sensing and wireless networking capabilities.

sources.title: Smart-Its: computers for artifacts in the physical world
sources.publisher: Communications of the ACM (ACM), Vol. 48, No. 3

sources.title: Smart-Its Friends: A Technique for Users to Easily Establish Connections between Smart Artefacts

interactions.title: Shake two objects together to pair them
interactions.description: Hold two Smart-Its-equipped artifacts in one hand and shake them; their accelerometers register the same motion signature and the pair connects, so only the objects you deliberately joined start talking to each other.

interactions.title: A chemical drum that warns when stored next to the wrong neighbor
interactions.description: Augmented containers carry ranging sensors and share what they hold; set two incompatible chemicals side by side and the drums compare type and mass with nearby containers and raise an alert about the hazardous arrangement.

interactions.title: Find a misplaced personal object
interactions.description: Clip a Smart-Its module onto a everyday item so it can be tracked, and the once-dumb object becomes locatable when it goes missing.

interactions.title: A tamper-evident electronic seal on physical goods
interactions.description: Attach an eSeal Smart-Its to a sealed item; the module continuously asserts the item's authenticity and physical integrity, flagging if the goods are opened or interfered with in transit.

interactions.title: Cups that infer a meeting is happening
interactions.description: Embed modules with accelerometer, thermometer, and proximity sensing into ordinary cups; when several warm cups gather close together, the objects read the situation as people meeting at that spot.

interactions.title: An exhibition hall turned reactive overnight
interactions.description: At SIGGRAPH 2003 the team rapidly embedded a network of Smart-Its into tables, chairs, and smaller artifacts, converting an ordinary exhibition space into an interactive environment that responded to how visitors used the furniture.

interactions.title: Pair a tool to its toolbox by shaking them as one
interactions.description: Give a hand tool and its case each a module, then shake them together to bond them; later the case notices when its shake-partner has wandered off and reminds you before you leave it behind.

interactions.title: A medicine bottle that compares notes with its shelf
interactions.description: Using the same proximity-and-context sharing as the chemical-drum demo, a pill bottle could check the temperature and identity of bottles around it and warn when a heat-sensitive medication is shelved somewhere too warm.
